paper_title: Vegetation Reconstruction From Siberia and the Tibetan Plateau Using Modern Analogue Technique–Comparing Sedimentary (Ancient) DNA and Pollen Data

methods.description: Process for determining the dominant vegetation type surrounding modern sampling sites using a ring-buffer analysis based on vegetation atlases.

steps.description: The dominant vegetation type surrounding each sampled lake was extracted from a site-specific ring-buffer using the "buffer()" function in the "raster" package (Hijmans, 2020).
steps.searchable_summary: Create spatial buffer around points using R raster package
outputs: Spatial buffer polygons for each site

steps.description: The dominant vegetation type surrounding each sampled lake was extracted from a site-specific ring-buffer...
steps.searchable_summary: Extract dominant feature from spatial layer within buffer polygon
inputs: Vegetation map layer (derived from atlases of China and Russia)
outputs: Dominant vegetation type for each modern site

methods.method_name: DNA Metabarcoding Data Processing and Filtering
methods.description: Procedures for processing raw DNA metabarcoding data using OBITools, taxonomic assignment against reference databases, and filtering based on sequence abundance, identity, and ecological relevance to produce a curated list of taxa for each sample.

steps.description: We used the OBITools package (Boyer et al., 2016) to analyse the DNA metabarcoding data.
steps.searchable_summary: Process raw DNA metabarcoding data using OBITools
outputs: Processed sequence data (e.g., demultiplexed, merged reads)

steps.description: ...which were converted for usage with the "ecotag" function implemented in OBITools (Boyer et al., 2016).
steps.searchable_summary: Format reference sequence databases for OBITools ecotag
inputs: Arctic and Boreal vascular plant and bryophyte reference library
European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL) Nucleotide Database

steps.description: For the taxonomic assignment we applied two publicly available reference databases... which were converted for usage with the "ecotag" function implemented in OBITools (Boyer et al., 2016).
steps.searchable_summary: Assign taxonomy to DNA sequences using OBITools ecotag and reference databases

steps.description: To further improve the quality of the modern sedDNA and sedaDNA data, sequences occurring <10 times in each sediment sample were ignored.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter DNA sequences based on minimum count per sample

steps.description: We only include terrestrial seed plants (Spermatophyta) sequences that had a 100% identity match to each of the references databases...
steps.searchable_summary: Filter DNA sequences by taxonomic group and identity match percentage
outputs: Filtered sequence data (taxonomic group and identity)

steps.description: ...and occurred at least in two independent PCR reactions. Since PCR replicates of samples from Omoloy lakes were amplified with same tagcombination, those sequences that were present in one sediment sample were kept.
steps.searchable_summary: Filter DNA sequences based on occurrence in multiple PCR replicates, with specific exception for Omoloy lakes

steps.description: Wetland taxa (e.g., Carex aquatilis, Comarum palustre, Sium suave) were excluded from all datasets.
steps.searchable_summary: Exclude specified wetland taxa from DNA sequence data
inputs: List of wetland taxa to exclude

steps.description: Furthermore, to avoid false positives (Ficetola et al., 2015), we excluded 0.3% taxa that should not be naturally found in our study area according to our vegetation surveys, the vegetation atlases of China and Russia, or iFlora of China (v. 2019, Brach andSong, 2006 1 ).
steps.searchable_summary: Exclude taxa from DNA sequence data based on geographical/ecological expectation
inputs: Vegetation atlases of China and Russia
outputs: Final curated DNA sequence data (sedDNA/sedaDNA) for analysis

methods.description: Using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve analysis on modern training sets (sedDNA and pollen) to determine optimal dissimilarity thresholds (d crit) for identifying good analogues in the Modern Analogue Technique (MAT).

steps.description: Prepare modern sedDNA training set data.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare modern sedDNA data for ROC analysis
inputs: Filtered modern sedDNA data (from DNA Metabarcoding Data Processing and Filtering)
Vegetation type information for modern sites (from Modern Site Vegetation Type Determination)
outputs: Structured modern sedDNA training set data suitable for ROC analysis

steps.description: For the modern sedDNA... training-sets... the ROC curve analyses give the d crit of 0.930...
steps.searchable_summary: Perform ROC curve analysis on modern sedDNA training data
inputs: Structured modern sedDNA training set data (from Step 1)
outputs: ROC curve analysis results for sedDNA

steps.description: ...the ROC curve analyses give the d crit of 0.930...
steps.searchable_summary: Extract optimal dissimilarity threshold (d crit) from ROC analysis results for sedDNA
inputs: ROC curve analysis results for sedDNA (from Step 2)
outputs: Optimal d crit value for sedDNA MAT (0.930)

steps.description: Prepare modern pollen training set data.
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare modern pollen data for ROC analysis
inputs: Vegetation type information for modern sites (from Modern Site Vegetation Type Determination)
outputs: Structured modern pollen training set data suitable for ROC analysis

steps.description: For the modern... pollen training-sets... the ROC curve analyses give the d crit of... 0.289,
steps.searchable_summary: Perform ROC curve analysis on modern pollen training data
inputs: Structured modern pollen training set data (from Step 4)
outputs: ROC curve analysis results for pollen

steps.description: ...the ROC curve analyses give the d crit of... 0.289,
steps.searchable_summary: Extract optimal dissimilarity threshold (d crit) from ROC analysis results for pollen
inputs: ROC curve analysis results for pollen (from Step 5)
outputs: Optimal d crit value for pollen MAT (0.289)
